flashcards.back: A day on which the portfolio's actual loss exceeds the loss predicted by the VaR model at its stated confidence level.

flashcards.front: Under a correctly calibrated VaR model, what should the long-run failure rate N/T converge to?
flashcards.back: To p = 1 − c, the probability of an exception implied by the model's confidence level c.

flashcards.front: Why does the assumption of a static portfolio complicate backtesting?
flashcards.back: VaR is computed assuming the current portfolio stays fixed over the horizon, but the real portfolio (and its P&L) changes continuously, so actual P&L reflects a different portfolio than the one the VaR estimate was based on.

flashcards.front: Give some items, besides market-risk-factor moves, that show up in a bank's actual daily P&L but not in its VaR model.
flashcards.back: Trading commissions and fees, net interest income, bid-ask spread effects, and gains/losses from intraday trades opened and closed the same day.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between "actual" and "cleaned" (hypothetical) returns in backtesting?
flashcards.back: Actual returns are the P&L the bank really reported, including fees, commissions, and intraday trading effects. Cleaned returns strip those out so the P&L reflects only mark-to-market moves on the static end-of-day portfolio.

flashcards.front: Write the z-score test statistic for backtesting and its 95% critical value.
flashcards.back: z = (N − pT) / √(p(1−p)T); reject the hypothesis that the model is unbiased if |z| > 1.96.

flashcards.front: In backtesting, what is a Type I error?
flashcards.back: Rejecting a VaR model that is actually correctly calibrated — a false alarm.

flashcards.front: In backtesting, what is a Type II error?
flashcards.back: Failing to reject (i.e., accepting) a VaR model that is actually mis-calibrated.

flashcards.front: What does Kupiec's LR_uc test, and what is its critical value?
flashcards.back: It tests "unconditional coverage" — whether the overall exception rate matches p — via a likelihood ratio statistic that is chi-squared(1) distributed under the null; reject if LR_uc > 3.84.

flashcards.front: Why is backtesting a 99% VaR model statistically harder than backtesting a 95% VaR model with the same sample size?
flashcards.back: The 99% model has far fewer expected exceptions (e.g., 2.5 vs. 12.5 in a 250-day sample), leaving less data to distinguish a correct model from an incorrect one, so both Type I and Type II error rates stay relatively high.

flashcards.front: What does conditional coverage testing add beyond unconditional coverage testing?
flashcards.back: It checks whether exceptions are serially independent (not clustered in time), not just whether the total exception count is right.

flashcards.front: What does LR_ind test, and what is its critical value?
flashcards.back: Christoffersen's test of serial independence of exceptions; chi-squared(1) distributed under the null, so reject independence if LR_ind > 3.84.

flashcards.front: How is the conditional coverage statistic LR_cc constructed, and what is its critical value?
flashcards.back: LR_cc = LR_uc + LR_ind, which is chi-squared(2) distributed under the joint null; reject the model if LR_cc > 5.99.

flashcards.front: Under Basel rules, what are the exception-count boundaries for the green, yellow, and red zones over a 250-day window at 99% confidence?
flashcards.back: Green = 0–4 exceptions (multiplier k=3, no penalty); Yellow = 5–9 exceptions (k rises by 0.40–0.85, subject to supervisor discretion); Red = 10+ exceptions (k automatically rises by 1, to k+1=4).

flashcards.front: What are the four categories regulators use to explain yellow-zone exceptions, and which ones trigger a penalty?
flashcards.back: (1) Basic integrity of the model is lacking — penalty applies; (2) model accuracy needs improvement — penalty applies; (3) intraday trading activity — penalty considered; (4) bad luck / volatile markets — no penalty mandated.

choices: A day where the model's VaR forecast exceeds a regulatory cap
A day where the portfolio's actual loss exceeds the VaR prediction for that day
A day where trading volume exceeds its historical average
A day where the portfolio's value increases beyond expectations
questions.explanation: An exception (or exceedance/breach) occurs when actual losses are larger than the loss predicted by the VaR model for that day.

questions.q: For a VaR model with confidence level c that is correctly calibrated, what should the long-run failure rate N/T converge to?
choices: c
c squared
questions.explanation: The probability of an exception is p = 1 − c, so the observed failure rate N/T should converge to p as the sample size T grows.

questions.q: Which statement best describes why a static-portfolio assumption complicates backtesting?
choices: VaR is calculated for the portfolio as it stands at one point in time and assumes it stays fixed, but the real portfolio changes continuously, so actual P&L reflects a different portfolio than the one VaR was based on
VaR models cannot be computed for portfolios that contain more than one asset
Static portfolios always have lower VaR than dynamic ones
Backtesting requires the portfolio to be liquidated before each test
questions.explanation: VaR is a snapshot prediction for the current, fixed portfolio, but real portfolios are continuously traded, creating a mismatch between the prediction and the realized P&L.

questions.q: In backtesting terminology, what is a "cleaned" (hypothetical) return series?
choices: The P&L including all fees, commissions, and intraday trading gains
The P&L recalculated to reflect only mark-to-market changes on the static end-of-day portfolio, with fees, commissions, and intraday trading effects stripped out
The P&L reported directly by the trading desk without any adjustment
The loss figure predicted by the VaR model itself
questions.explanation: Cleaned returns remove non-mark-to-market items (fees, commissions, intraday trading effects) so the series reflects only how the static end-of-day portfolio responded to market moves — directly comparable to what the VaR model is trying to predict.

questions.q: A bank's one-day 95% VaR model is backtested over T = 250 days and produces N = 20 exceptions. What is the z-statistic, and what does it imply?
choices: z ≈ 0.87; fail to reject — the model appears well-calibrated
z ≈ 1.45; fail to reject — the model appears well-calibrated
z ≈ 2.18; reject — the model appears to understate risk
z ≈ 3.50; reject — the model appears to overstate risk
questions.explanation: p = 0.05, pT = 12.5, p(1−p)T = 11.875 so √(p(1−p)T) ≈ 3.446. z = (20 − 12.5)/3.446 ≈ 2.18 > 1.96, so the unbiasedness hypothesis is rejected — too many exceptions suggests the model understates risk.

questions.q: In the context of VaR backtesting, a Type I error refers to:
choices: Rejecting a VaR model that is actually correctly calibrated
Failing to reject a VaR model that is actually mis-calibrated
Using the wrong holding period in the VaR calculation
Reporting more exceptions than were actually observed
questions.explanation: A Type I error is a false alarm: concluding a correctly calibrated model is bad, based on a sample that happened to show an unusually high (or low) exception count.

questions.q: In the context of VaR backtesting, a Type II error refers to:
choices: Rejecting a VaR model that is actually correctly calibrated
Failing to reject (i.e., accepting) a VaR model that is actually mis-calibrated
Choosing too high a VaR confidence level
Computing the failure rate incorrectly
questions.explanation: A Type II error means an inaccurate model passes the backtest because its sample happened to produce a plausible-looking exception count.

questions.q: The chi-squared(1) critical value used for Kupiec's LR_uc test (3.84) is numerically equal to 1.96 squared. Why?
choices: Because a chi-squared(1) random variable is the square of a standard normal random variable, and 1.96 is the standard normal 95% critical value
Because both tests always use exactly the same sample size
Because LR_uc and the z-test are mathematically identical formulas
It is a coincidence with no statistical basis
questions.explanation: A chi-squared distribution with 1 degree of freedom is, by definition, the distribution of the square of a standard normal variable, so the two tests' critical values are mathematically linked: 1.96² = 3.8416 ≈ 3.84.

questions.q: A bank's one-day 99% VaR model is backtested over T = 250 days and produces N = 8 exceptions. Approximately what is LR_uc, and what is the conclusion at the 95% test confidence level?
choices: LR_uc ≈ 0.03; fail to reject
LR_uc ≈ 1.50; fail to reject
LR_uc ≈ 3.84; borderline, inconclusive
LR_uc ≈ 7.73; reject — the model likely understates risk
questions.explanation: With p = 0.01, T = 250, N = 8, evaluating LR_uc = −2 ln[(1−p)^(T−N) p^N / (1−N/T)^(T−N) (N/T)^N] gives approximately 7.73, which exceeds the chi-squared(1) critical value of 3.84, so the model is rejected — 8 exceptions is too many for a 99% model over 250 days.

questions.q: Relative to Kupiec's unconditional coverage test, what additional question does Christoffersen's LR_ind statistic answer?
choices: Whether the total number of exceptions matches the expected count
Whether exceptions cluster together in time (serial dependence) rather than occurring independently
Whether the VaR model used the correct confidence level
Whether the portfolio's composition changed during the testing window
questions.explanation: LR_ind tests serial independence of the exception sequence — whether exceptions tend to follow other exceptions more (or less) often than chance would predict, which LR_uc cannot detect since it only looks at the total count.

questions.q: How is the conditional coverage statistic LR_cc constructed, and what is its critical value at 95% confidence?
choices: LR_cc = LR_uc × LR_ind; critical value 3.84
LR_cc = LR_uc + LR_ind; critical value 5.99 (chi-squared, 2 degrees of freedom)
LR_cc = LR_uc − LR_ind; critical value 1.96
LR_cc = LR_ind / LR_uc; critical value 0.05
questions.explanation: LR_cc sums the unconditional coverage and independence statistics; since each is chi-squared(1) and they are independent under the joint null, their sum is chi-squared(2), with a 95% critical value of 5.99.

questions.q: Under the Basel framework (99% VaR, 250-day window), a bank reports 7 exceptions. Which zone does this fall into?
choices: Green zone — no penalty
Yellow zone — supervisor evaluates the cause of the exceptions
Red zone — automatic penalty
Outside the defined zones; the test is inconclusive
questions.explanation: 5–9 exceptions over 250 days at 99% confidence falls in the yellow zone, where the supervisor investigates the cause before deciding whether a penalty applies.

questions.q: Under the same Basel framework, a bank reports 11 exceptions over 250 days. What happens?
choices: Green zone — no action
Yellow zone — supervisor discretion applies
Red zone — the capital multiplier automatically increases by 1
The model automatically passes since more exceptions imply more conservative VaR
questions.explanation: 10 or more exceptions over 250 days places the bank in the red zone, where the multiplier add-on (k) automatically increases by 1, regardless of the underlying cause.

questions.q: Which of the four yellow-zone cause categories does Basel's framework NOT require a penalty for?
choices: Basic integrity of the model is lacking
Model accuracy needs improvement
Intraday trading activity caused the exceptions
Bad luck — unusually volatile market conditions
questions.explanation: Bad luck / volatile markets is treated as a plausible outcome even for a correctly specified model, so Basel's framework does not mandate a penalty in this case, unlike the integrity, accuracy, and (to a lesser extent) intraday-trading categories.

questions.q: Why might lowering the required VaR confidence level from 99% to 95% (paired with a larger capital multiplier) improve the statistical properties of the backtest?
choices: It increases the expected number of exceptions (from about 2.5 to about 12.5 over 250 days), giving the test more power to distinguish correct from incorrect models and improving both Type I and Type II error rates
It removes the need for backtesting entirely
It guarantees zero exceptions regardless of model quality
It eliminates the chi-squared distribution requirement for LR_uc
questions.explanation: A lower confidence level produces more expected exceptions for the same sample size, which gives the test more statistical power — both Type I and Type II error rates can be reduced relative to the 99%/250-day setup, where only 2.5 exceptions are expected.
